{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=20qq-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=20qq-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=20qq-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=20qq-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=20qq-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=20qq-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128年}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907年}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=20qq-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano
