{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=draft|12=draft}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=draft|12=draft}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft|12=draft}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft|12=draft}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|12=draft|12=draft}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体|12=draft|12=draft}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸|12=draft|12=draft}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft|12=draft}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft|12=draft}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=draft|12=draft}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|12=draft|12=draft}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907th year|12=draft|12=draft}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft|12=draft}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=draft|12=draft}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft|12=draft}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamikura Shrine}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|天照大神|" to "|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=draft|12=draft}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=draft|12=draft}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft|12=draft}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft|12=draft}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|12=draft|12=draft}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体|1=Shintai|12=draft|12=draft}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸|12=draft|12=draft}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft|12=draft}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft|12=draft}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=draft|12=draft}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|12=draft|12=draft}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907th year|12=draft|12=draft}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft|12=draft}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=draft|12=draft}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft|12=draft}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|12=draft|12=draft}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamikura Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|12=draft}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}} | Established = {{ill|128 AD|ja|128th year|12=draft}} (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=draft}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|ja|新宮市|12=draft}}, Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft}}, part of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|ja|紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道|12=draft}}. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|12=draft}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} by the patronage of {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}}. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体|1=Shintai|12=draft}}. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of {{ill|dōtaku|ja|銅鐸|12=draft}}, indicating the site's origin in {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, this location is associated with the legendary {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}}, who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=draft}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=draft}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=draft}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|12=draft}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in {{ill|1907|ja|1907th year|12=draft}} during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=draft}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * {{ill|JR Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}: From {{ill|Shingū Station|ja|新宮駅|12=draft}}, it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|12=draft}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|12=draft}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamikura Shrine}}

revision.comment: 67 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:神倉神社 up to rev 103649309
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=神倉神社 |画像=[[ファイル:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
御神体の巨岩・ゴトビキ岩と拝殿 |所在地=和歌山県新宮市神倉1-13-8 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神=[[天照大神]]、[[高倉下|高倉下命]] |社格=旧[[村社]] |創建=[[128年]]（[[景行天皇]]58年） |例祭=2月6日（[[御燈祭]]） |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=神倉神社|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} '''神倉神社'''（かみくらじんじゃ、かんのくらじんじゃ）は[[和歌山県]][[新宮市]]の[[神社]]。[[熊野三山]]の一山である[[熊野速玉大社]]の[[摂末社|摂社]]。境内地は国の[[史跡]]「熊野三山」の一部、および[[世界遺産]]「[[紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道]]」の一部である。 == 磐座信仰の社 == [[ファイル:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|参道麓の鳥居]] 神倉神社は、[[熊野速玉大社]]の摂社である。[[新宮市]]中心市街地北西部にある[[千穂ヶ峯]]の支ピーク、神倉山（かみくらさん、かんのくらやま、標高120メートル）に鎮座し、境内外縁は直ちに断崖絶壁になっている。山上へは、[[源頼朝]]が寄進したと伝えられる、急勾配の鎌倉積み石段538段を登らなければならない。 山上にはゴトビキ岩（「琴引岩」とも。ゴトビキとはヒキガエルをあらわす新宮の方言）と呼ばれる巨岩が[[神体]]として祀られている「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編[1985: 294]{{Cite web|和書|url=https://nankikumanogeo.jp/cms/wp_geopark/wp-content/uploads/panf4.pdf|title=南紀熊野ジオパーク |publisher=南紀熊野ジオパーク推進協議会 |accessdate=2022-08-25}}。この岩の根元を支える袈裟岩といわれる岩の周辺には経塚が発見されており、[[平安時代]]の経筒が多数発掘され、そのさらに下層からは銅鐸片や滑石製模造品が出土していることから、神倉神社の起源は[[磐座]]信仰から発したと考えられている。 神倉神社の創建年代は[[128年]]頃といわれているが、神話時代にさかのぼる古くからの伝承がある。『[[古事記]]』『[[日本書紀]]』によれば、神倉山は、[[神武天皇]]が東征の際に登った天磐盾（あまのいわたて{{Cite book |和書 |translator=[[宇治谷孟]] |title=日本書紀（上） 全現代語訳 |year=1988 |publisher=[[講談社]] |series=[[講談社学術文庫]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}}、あめのいわたて{{Cite book |和書 |editor=[[和田萃]]編 |title=熊野権現 |year=1988 |publisher=[[筑摩書房]] |isbn=4-480-85421-5 |pages=7・163頁}}）の山であるという「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編[1985: 294]平凡社編[1997: 191]。このとき、天照大神の子孫の[[高倉下|高倉下命]]が神武に神剣を奉げ、これを得た神武は、天照大神の遣わした[[八咫烏]]の道案内で軍を進め、熊野・大和を制圧したとされている。しかし、「熊野権現御垂迹縁起」（『[[長寛勘文]]』所収）には神剣と神倉山を結びつける記述はないことから、天磐盾を神倉山と結びつける所説は[[鎌倉時代]]以降に現れたものと考えられている。 === 歴史 === [[熊野信仰]]が盛んになると、熊野権現が諸国遍歴の末に、熊野で最初に降臨した場所であると説かれるようになった（「[[長寛勘文|熊野権現垂迹縁起]]」）。この説に従えば、熊野三所大神がどこよりも最初に降臨したのはこの地であり宮家[1992: 2]、そのことから熊野根本神蔵権現あるいは熊野速玉大社奥院と称された。平安時代以降には、神倉山を拠点として修行する修験者が集うようになり、熊野参詣記にもいく度かその名が登場する。『[[平家物語]]』巻10の[[平維盛]]熊野参詣の記事に登場するほか、[[応永]]34年（[[1427年]]）には、[[足利義満]]の側室北野殿の参詣記に「神の蔵」参詣の記述が見られる。 鎌倉時代の[[建長]]3年（[[1251年]]）2月14日には火災により焼失したが、執権の[[北条時頼]]より助成を与えられて再建された。中世の神倉神社は、神倉聖と称される社僧のほか、その下役の残位坊、[[妙心寺 (新宮市)|妙心寺]]（妙心尼寺）・華厳院・宝積院・三学院の神倉本願四ヵ寺が運営にあたり（『[[紀伊続風土記]]』）、中の地蔵堂・参道・曼荼羅堂などの維持管理にあたった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の動乱による荒廃の後はもっぱら妙心尼寺が勧進権を掌握した。[[享禄]]4年（[[1531年]]）付の古記録「神倉再造由緒」によると、神倉山の神社仏閣は[[持統天皇]]の時代に[[裸行]]上人により建立されたが、その後荒廃したため、[[延徳]]元年（[[1489年]]）に妙心尼寺の妙順尼が神倉神社の再興のための[[勧進]]を行い、さらに[[大永]]年間（[[1521年]]-[[1528年]]）から享禄4年まで弟子の祐珍尼らとともに諸国を巡って奉加を募り、これによって再興を成し遂げた（『妙心寺文書』）新宮市[1984: 971]。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]から近世初期にかけても度々災害に見舞われているが、なかでも[[天正]]16年（[[1588年]]）には、[[豊臣秀長]]の木材奉行によって放火され、境内がことごとく焼失した。翌年には祐心尼のほか、金蔵坊祐信（当山派）および熊野新宮の楽浄坊行満（本山派）の2人の修験者の協力を得て西国9か国に勧進に赴いている新宮市[1984: 972]。 近世以降は、[[紀州徳川家]]や、新宮領主の[[浅野氏]]・[[水野氏]]の崇敬をあつめた。[[慶長]]7年([[1602年]]）には浅野氏より社領として63[[石]]を与えられたほか、[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）には一年の祈祷料として、米3石6[[斗]]と燈明料米1石8斗が与えられた平凡社編[1997: 192]との記録が見える。 [[ファイル:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|拝殿]] 『紀伊続風土記』が伝えるところによると、近世の境内には社殿と並宮のほか、崖上に[[懸造]]の拝殿があり、大黒天を祀る御供所、末社として満山社、子安社、中の地蔵堂などがあったが、[[明治3年]]（[[1870年]]）の台風で倒壊し、その後荒廃したため、[[1907年]]（[[明治]]40年）には熊野速玉大社に合祀された。しかし、[[1918年]]（[[大正]]7年）、岩下に祠を再建したのを手始めに、[[昭和]]期に[[社務所]]、鳥居などが再建された。現在は社務所に常駐の[[神職]]は居らず、熊野速玉大社の境外社の扱いである。[[朱印 (神社仏閣)|朱印]]や御札などは熊野速玉大社の社務所で取り扱っている。御朱印には「[[熊野三山]]元宮」と記載されている。 == 文化財 == === 国指定史跡 === {{See also|千穂ヶ峯}} 神倉神社境内は、熊野速玉大社境内地の一部（神倉山 附石段 「下馬」標石）として国の史跡「熊野三山」{{国指定文化財等データベース2 |name=熊野三山 |register=401 |item=3364 |year=2000 |jyear=平成12 |month=11 |date=2 |year2=2002 |jyear2=平成14 |month2=12 |date2=19 |additional=分離・追加指定・名称変更 |cat1=史跡 |accessdate=2010-06-12 }}に含まれる{{Cite web|和書|url=http://wave.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/bunkazai/cgi/search.cgi?action=view_id&view_id=447|title=熊野三山熊野速玉大社境内（神倉山）|publisher=和歌山県|work=[http://wave.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/bunkazai/index.html わかやま文化財ガイド]|accessdate=2013-01-29}}。史跡「熊野三山」は世界遺産「[[紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道]]」（[[2004年]]〈平成16年〉7月登録）の構成資産の一部{{Cite book ja-jp|author=世界遺産登録推進三県協議会|year=2005|title=世界遺産 紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道|publisher=世界遺産登録推進三県協議会（和歌山県・奈良県・三重県）}}、pp.39,75。 === 和歌山県指定史跡 === [[ファイル:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|御燈祭では、大勢の男たちがこの荒々しい石段を駆け下りる]] 神倉山の石段 附 「下馬」標石は、神倉山山麓から山上境内へ続く石段、および神倉神社入口にかかる太鼓橋の傍らに立つ標石である。 石段は538段を算し、自然石（[[花崗岩]]）を組み合わせて築かれたもので、幅は平均約4メートル、高さと奥行きは一定ではないが、おおよそ高さ25センチメートル、奥行きは30から40センチメートルである新宮市教育委員会・新宮市文化財審議会編[1990: 30]。一般には[[建久]]4年（[[1193年]]）に源頼朝が寄進したと伝えられるが、わずかに『[[熊野年代記]]』の記事がその旨を伝えるに過ぎない。 「下馬」標石は寛文12年銘のあるもので、黒雲母花崗斑岩製、高さ1.59メートル、幅43センチメートル、厚さ17センチ、同じ材質で地上高26センチメートル、幅80センチメートルの台石上に立っている新宮市教育委員会・新宮市文化財審議会編[1990: 33]。刻銘によれば、[[奥州]]は南部志和郡の人物が、子孫繁栄を祈念して熊野参詣7度を達成したことを記念したものとあり、奥州の熊野信仰を今日に伝えている。 === 新宮市指定有形民俗文化財 === 境内にはそのほかに、新宮市指定の有形民俗文化財3件があり、[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）3月25日に指定されたこの節、有形民俗文化財の節は新宮市教育委員会・新宮市文化財審議会編[1990: 100-101、112]による。。'''神倉神社の手水鉢'''1基は、[[新宮城]]第2代城主の[[水野重良]]から寄進されたもので、幅1.9メートル、奥行き91.0センチメートル、高さ60.0センチメートル、黒雲母花崗斑岩の巨大な一枚岩を刻んで仕上げた[[江戸時代]]のもので、[[寛永]]8年2月の銘がある。'''神倉神社の手水船'''1基は、[[1956年]]（昭和31年）からの山上境内における鳥居玉垣補修工事の際に発掘されたもので、幅88.8センチメートル、奥行き53.0センチメートル、高さ34.0センチメートル、南北朝時代に作成された砂岩製・舟形の手水鉢で、現在は熊野速玉大社境内に移設されている。縁の三方は波型で一方だけが直線という形状で、慶安4年の施入銘がある。'''奉八度参詣碑'''は久慈八日町（[[岩手県]][[久慈市]]）の住人、吉田金右衛門なる人物が、熊野詣8回を遂げた記念として、[[宝永]]5年（[[1708年]]）に奉納したもので、困難をおして参詣を遂げた奥州人の熊野信仰の記念碑である。 File:Kamikura-jinja, chouzu.jpg|手水鉢 [[ファイル:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|サムネイル|御燈祭（おとうまつり）]] == 御燈祭 == {{See|御燈祭}} 熊野に春を呼ぶ男の火まつりとして毎年2月6日夜に斎行される。約2600年前、[[神武天皇東征]]の際に祭神・[[高倉下命]](たかくらじのみこと)が松明を持って案内したことに由来するとの故事もあるが、起源は『[[熊野年代記]]』に[[敏達天皇]]三年（574年）とされる{{Cite journal|和書 |author=守安敏久 |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=中上健次『火まつり』 ―映画から小説へ― |journal=宇都宮大学教育学部紀要. 第1部 |ISSN=03871266 |publisher=宇都宮大学教育学部 |issue=61 |pages=17-28 |hdl=10241/00004566}}。 == 交通機関 == [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[紀勢本線]][[新宮駅]]下車 [[熊野交通]]の路線バスで「裁判所前」下車、または徒歩15分。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist|30em}} == 文献 == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編|year=1985|title=和歌山県|publisher=角川書店|series=角川日本地名大辞典30|isbn=404001300X}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=五来 重|year=1976|chapter=吉野・熊野修験道の成立と展開|editor2=五来|title=吉野・熊野信仰の研究|publisher=名著出版|series=山岳宗教史研究叢書4|pages=13-46}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=新宮市編|year=1984|title=新宮市誌|publisher=新興出版社}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=新宮市教育委員会・新宮市文化財審議会編|year=1990|title=新宮市の文化財|publisher=新宮市教育委員会}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=平凡社編|year=1997|title=大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典|publisher=平凡社|isbn=4582134025}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=宮家 準|year=1992|title=熊野修験|publisher=吉川弘文館|series=日本歴史叢書|isbn=4642066497}} == 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野速玉大社]] * [[阿須賀神社]] * [[妙心寺 (新宮市)]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 摂社神倉神社]（[http://kumanohayatama.jp/ 熊野速玉大社]公式ウェブサイト内） * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/075.html 熊野速玉大社境内（神倉山）] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/index.html 和歌山県フォト博物館] {{紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かみくらしんしや}} [[Category:熊野三山]] [[Category:和歌山県にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:新宮市の歴史]] [[Category:新宮市の建築物]] [[Category:熊野信仰]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[神倉神社]] to [[Kamikura Shrine]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=神倉神社 |画像=[[ファイル:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
御神体の巨岩・ゴトビキ岩と拝殿 |所在地=和歌山県新宮市神倉1-13-8 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神=[[天照大神]]、[[高倉下|高倉下命]] |社格=旧[[村社]] |創建=[[128年]]（[[景行天皇]]58年） |例祭=2月6日（[[御燈祭]]） |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=神倉神社|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} '''神倉神社'''（かみくらじんじゃ、かんのくらじんじゃ）は[[和歌山県]][[新宮市]]の[[神社]]。[[熊野三山]]の一山である[[熊野速玉大社]]の[[摂末社|摂社]]。境内地は国の[[史跡]]「熊野三山」の一部、および[[世界遺産]]「[[紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道]]」の一部である。 == 磐座信仰の社 == [[ファイル:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|参道麓の鳥居]] 神倉神社は、[[熊野速玉大社]]の摂社である。[[新宮市]]中心市街地北西部にある[[千穂ヶ峯]]の支ピーク、神倉山（かみくらさん、かんのくらやま、標高120メートル）に鎮座し、境内外縁は直ちに断崖絶壁になっている。山上へは、[[源頼朝]]が寄進したと伝えられる、急勾配の鎌倉積み石段538段を登らなければならない。 |publisher=南紀熊野ジオパーク推進協議会 |accessdate=2022-08-25}}。この岩の根元を支える袈裟岩といわれる岩の周辺には経塚が発見されており、[[平安時代]]の経筒が多数発掘され、そのさらに下層からは銅鐸片や滑石製模造品が出土していることから、神倉神社の起源は[[磐座]]信仰から発したと考えられている。 神倉神社の創建年代は[[128年]]頃といわれているが、神話時代にさかのぼる古くからの伝承がある。『[[古事記]]』『[[日本書紀]]』によれば、神倉山は、[[神武天皇]]が東征の際に登った天磐盾（あまのいわたて{{Cite book |和書 |translator=[[宇治谷孟]] |title=日本書紀（上） 全現代語訳 |year=1988 |publisher=[[講談社]] |series=[[講談社学術文庫]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}}、あめのいわたて{{Cite book |和書 |editor=[[和田萃]]編 |title=熊野権現 |year=1988 |publisher=[[筑摩書房]] |isbn=4-480-85421-5 |pages=7・163頁}}）の山であるという「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編[1985: 294]平凡社編[1997: 191]。このとき、天照大神の子孫の[[高倉下|高倉下命]]が神武に神剣を奉げ、これを得た神武は、天照大神の遣わした[[八咫烏]]の道案内で軍を進め、熊野・大和を制圧したとされている。しかし、「熊野権現御垂迹縁起」（『[[長寛勘文]]』所収）には神剣と神倉山を結びつける記述はないことから、天磐盾を神倉山と結びつける所説は[[鎌倉時代]]以降に現れたものと考えられている。 === 歴史 === [[熊野信仰]]が盛んになると、熊野権現が諸国遍歴の末に、熊野で最初に降臨した場所であると説かれるようになった（「[[長寛勘文|熊野権現垂迹縁起]]」）。この説に従えば、熊野三所大神がどこよりも最初に降臨したのはこの地であり宮家[1992: 2]、そのことから熊野根本神蔵権現あるいは熊野速玉大社奥院と称された。平安時代以降には、神倉山を拠点として修行する修験者が集うようになり、熊野参詣記にもいく度かその名が登場する。『[[平家物語]]』巻10の[[平維盛]]熊野参詣の記事に登場するほか、[[応永]]34年（[[1427年]]）には、[[足利義満]]の側室北野殿の参詣記に「神の蔵」参詣の記述が見られる。 鎌倉時代の[[建長]]3年（[[1251年]]）2月14日には火災により焼失したが、執権の[[北条時頼]]より助成を与えられて再建された。中世の神倉神社は、神倉聖と称される社僧のほか、その下役の残位坊、[[妙心寺 (新宮市)|妙心寺]]（妙心尼寺）・華厳院・宝積院・三学院の神倉本願四ヵ寺が運営にあたり（『[[紀伊続風土記]]』）、中の地蔵堂・参道・曼荼羅堂などの維持管理にあたった。[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の動乱による荒廃の後はもっぱら妙心尼寺が勧進権を掌握した。[[享禄]]4年（[[1531年]]）付の古記録「神倉再造由緒」によると、神倉山の神社仏閣は[[持統天皇]]の時代に[[裸行]]上人により建立されたが、その後荒廃したため、[[延徳]]元年（[[1489年]]）に妙心尼寺の妙順尼が神倉神社の再興のための[[勧進]]を行い、さらに[[大永]]年間（[[1521年]]-[[1528年]]）から享禄4年まで弟子の祐珍尼らとともに諸国を巡って奉加を募り、これによって再興を成し遂げた（『妙心寺文書』）新宮市[1984: 971]。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]から近世初期にかけても度々災害に見舞われているが、なかでも[[天正]]16年（[[1588年]]）には、[[豊臣秀長]]の木材奉行によって放火され、境内がことごとく焼失した。翌年には祐心尼のほか、金蔵坊祐信（当山派）および熊野新宮の楽浄坊行満（本山派）の2人の修験者の協力を得て西国9か国に勧進に赴いている新宮市[1984: 972]。 近世以降は、[[紀州徳川家]]や、新宮領主の[[浅野氏]]・[[水野氏]]の崇敬をあつめた。[[慶長]]7年([[1602年]]）には浅野氏より社領として63[[石]]を与えられたほか、[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）には一年の祈祷料として、米3石6[[斗]]と燈明料米1石8斗が与えられた平凡社編[1997: 192]との記録が見える。 [[ファイル:Kamikura-jinja01s2048.jpg|thumb|拝殿]] 『紀伊続風土記』が伝えるところによると、近世の境内には社殿と並宮のほか、崖上に[[懸造]]の拝殿があり、大黒天を祀る御供所、末社として満山社、子安社、中の地蔵堂などがあったが、[[明治3年]]（[[1870年]]）の台風で倒壊し、その後荒廃したため、[[1907年]]（[[明治]]40年）には熊野速玉大社に合祀された。しかし、[[1918年]]（[[大正]]7年）、岩下に祠を再建したのを手始めに、[[昭和]]期に[[社務所]]、鳥居などが再建された。現在は社務所に常駐の[[神職]]は居らず、熊野速玉大社の境外社の扱いである。[[朱印 (神社仏閣)|朱印]]や御札などは熊野速玉大社の社務所で取り扱っている。御朱印には「[[熊野三山]]元宮」と記載されている。 == 文化財 == === 国指定史跡 === {{See also|千穂ヶ峯}} 神倉神社境内は、熊野速玉大社境内地の一部（神倉山 附石段 「下馬」標石）として国の史跡「熊野三山」{{国指定文化財等データベース2 |name=熊野三山 |register=401 |item=3364 |year=2000 |jyear=平成12 |month=11 |date=2 |year2=2002 |jyear2=平成14 |month2=12 |date2=19 |additional=分離・追加指定・名称変更 |cat1=史跡 |accessdate=2010-06-12 }}に含まれる{{Cite web|和書|url=http://wave.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/bunkazai/cgi/search.cgi?action=view_id&view_id=447|title=熊野三山熊野速玉大社境内（神倉山）|publisher=和歌山県|work=[http://wave.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/bunkazai/index.html わかやま文化財ガイド]|accessdate=2013-01-29}}。史跡「熊野三山」は世界遺産「[[紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道]]」（[[2004年]]〈平成16年〉7月登録）の構成資産の一部{{Cite book ja-jp|author=世界遺産登録推進三県協議会|year=2005|title=世界遺産 紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道|publisher=世界遺産登録推進三県協議会（和歌山県・奈良県・三重県）}}、pp.39,75。 === 和歌山県指定史跡 === [[ファイル:Kamikura-jinja13s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|御燈祭では、大勢の男たちがこの荒々しい石段を駆け下りる]] 神倉山の石段 附 「下馬」標石は、神倉山山麓から山上境内へ続く石段、および神倉神社入口にかかる太鼓橋の傍らに立つ標石である。 石段は538段を算し、自然石（[[花崗岩]]）を組み合わせて築かれたもので、幅は平均約4メートル、高さと奥行きは一定ではないが、おおよそ高さ25センチメートル、奥行きは30から40センチメートルである新宮市教育委員会・新宮市文化財審議会編[1990: 30]。一般には[[建久]]4年（[[1193年]]）に源頼朝が寄進したと伝えられるが、わずかに『[[熊野年代記]]』の記事がその旨を伝えるに過ぎない。 「下馬」標石は寛文12年銘のあるもので、黒雲母花崗斑岩製、高さ1.59メートル、幅43センチメートル、厚さ17センチ、同じ材質で地上高26センチメートル、幅80センチメートルの台石上に立っている新宮市教育委員会・新宮市文化財審議会編[1990: 33]。刻銘によれば、[[奥州]]は南部志和郡の人物が、子孫繁栄を祈念して熊野参詣7度を達成したことを記念したものとあり、奥州の熊野信仰を今日に伝えている。 === 新宮市指定有形民俗文化財 === 境内にはそのほかに、新宮市指定の有形民俗文化財3件があり、[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）3月25日に指定されたこの節、有形民俗文化財の節は新宮市教育委員会・新宮市文化財審議会編[1990: 100-101、112]による。。'''神倉神社の手水鉢'''1基は、[[新宮城]]第2代城主の[[水野重良]]から寄進されたもので、幅1.9メートル、奥行き91.0センチメートル、高さ60.0センチメートル、黒雲母花崗斑岩の巨大な一枚岩を刻んで仕上げた[[江戸時代]]のもので、[[寛永]]8年2月の銘がある。'''神倉神社の手水船'''1基は、[[1956年]]（昭和31年）からの山上境内における鳥居玉垣補修工事の際に発掘されたもので、幅88.8センチメートル、奥行き53.0センチメートル、高さ34.0センチメートル、南北朝時代に作成された砂岩製・舟形の手水鉢で、現在は熊野速玉大社境内に移設されている。縁の三方は波型で一方だけが直線という形状で、慶安4年の施入銘がある。'''奉八度参詣碑'''は久慈八日町（[[岩手県]][[久慈市]]）の住人、吉田金右衛門なる人物が、熊野詣8回を遂げた記念として、[[宝永]]5年（[[1708年]]）に奉納したもので、困難をおして参詣を遂げた奥州人の熊野信仰の記念碑である。 File:Kamikura-jinja, chouzu.jpg|手水鉢 [[ファイル:518wakayama-Kumano Oto Festival-xl.jpg|サムネイル|御燈祭（おとうまつり）]] == 御燈祭 == {{See|御燈祭}} 熊野に春を呼ぶ男の火まつりとして毎年2月6日夜に斎行される。約2600年前、[[神武天皇東征]]の際に祭神・[[高倉下命]](たかくらじのみこと)が松明を持って案内したことに由来するとの故事もあるが、起源は『[[熊野年代記]]』に[[敏達天皇]]三年（574年）とされる{{Cite journal|和書 |author=守安敏久 |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=中上健次『火まつり』 ―映画から小説へ― |journal=宇都宮大学教育学部紀要. 第1部 |ISSN=03871266 |publisher=宇都宮大学教育学部 |issue=61 |pages=17-28 |hdl=10241/00004566}}。 == 交通機関 == [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[紀勢本線]][[新宮駅]]下車 [[熊野交通]]の路線バスで「裁判所前」下車、または徒歩15分。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist|30em}} == 文献 == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編|year=1985|title=和歌山県|publisher=角川書店|series=角川日本地名大辞典30|isbn=404001300X}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=五来 重|year=1976|chapter=吉野・熊野修験道の成立と展開|editor2=五来|title=吉野・熊野信仰の研究|publisher=名著出版|series=山岳宗教史研究叢書4|pages=13-46}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=新宮市編|year=1984|title=新宮市誌|publisher=新興出版社}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=新宮市教育委員会・新宮市文化財審議会編|year=1990|title=新宮市の文化財|publisher=新宮市教育委員会}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=平凡社編|year=1997|title=大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典|publisher=平凡社|isbn=4582134025}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=宮家 準|year=1992|title=熊野修験|publisher=吉川弘文館|series=日本歴史叢書|isbn=4642066497}} == 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野速玉大社]] * [[阿須賀神社]] * [[妙心寺 (新宮市)]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 摂社神倉神社]（[http://kumanohayatama.jp/ 熊野速玉大社]公式ウェブサイト内） * [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/075.html 熊野速玉大社境内（神倉山）] - [http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/000200/photomuseum/index.html 和歌山県フォト博物館] {{紀伊山地の霊場と参詣道}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かみくらしんしや}} [[Category:熊野三山]] [[Category:和歌山県にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:新宮市の歴史]] [[Category:新宮市の建築物]] [[Category:熊野信仰]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|qq=}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}} | Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|qq=}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}} | Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:和歌山県にある国指定の史跡]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:和歌山県の旧村社

This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:和歌山県にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|qq=}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}} | Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:和歌山県にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:新宮市の建築物]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|qq=}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}} | Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:National historic sites in Wakayama prefecture]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:新宮市の建築物]] [[Category:新宮市の歴史]]
