This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:National historic sites in Wakayama prefecture]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:Buildings in Shingu, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Shingu, Wakayama]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|qq=}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}} | Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[en:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:National historic sites in Wakayama prefecture]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:Buildings in Shingu, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Shingu, Wakayama]] {{Commons category|Kamikura-jinja}} [[fr:Kamikura-jinja]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kamikura Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Wakayama]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Religious buildings in Shingū, Wakayama]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[Category:National historic sites in Wakayama prefecture]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:Buildings in Shingu, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Shingu, Wakayama]] {{Commons category|Kamikura-jinja}} [[fr:Kamikura-jinja]]

This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kamikura Shrine (神倉神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kamikura-jinja, shaden.jpg|250px]]
Sacred rock Gotobiki Rock and worship hall | location = [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan | Position = {{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], {{ill|Takakuraji|ja|高倉下命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakuraji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 高倉下}} | type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō) | Festival = February 6 ({{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}}) | Map = {{location map|Japan Wakayama|lat_deg=33|lat_min=43|lat_sec=20.1|lon_deg=135|lon_min=58|lon_sec=58.2|label=Kamikura Shrine|position=left|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}} }} The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]. == Sacred Rock Worship Shrine == [[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]] Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]]. The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship."Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294 According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}} == History == The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191 The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.Shingū City, 1984, p. 971 It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30 == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}} === Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site === The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide === Local Cultural Properties === Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990 == Oto Matsuri == {{See|Oto Matsuri}} The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}} == Access == The shrine is accessible by: * [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス}} bus to the "Court Office" stop. == References == {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City|year=1984|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Shingū City Education Committee|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū|publisher=Shingū City Education Committee}} * {{Cite book ja-jp|author=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Great Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii|publisher=Heibonsha}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] * {{ill|Asuka Shrine|ja|阿須賀神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|ja|妙心寺 (新宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshin-ji (Shingū)|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kamikura-jinja}} * [http://kumanohayatama.jp/?page_id=18 Kamikura Shrine] - Official site of Kumano Hayatama Taisha {{translated page|ja|神倉神社|version=103649309|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Kumano Sanzan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Subordinate shrines]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] }} [[ja:神倉神社]] [[sn:Kamikura Shrine]] [[sn:amikura Shrine]] [[Category:National historic sites in Wakayama prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Setsumatsusha]] [[Category:Buildings in Shingu, Wakayama]] [[Category:History of Shingu, Wakayama]] {{Commons category|Kamikura-jinja}} [[fr:Kamikura-jinja]]

The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]].

== Sacred Rock Worship Shrine ==

[[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]]
Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=|1=Mount Chihogamine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405828}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].

The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship.<ref name="Kadokawa_1985_294">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294</ref>

According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.<ref>{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}}</ref>

== History ==
The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|qid=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.<ref name="Heibon_1997_191">Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191</ref>

The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.<ref>Shingū City, 1984, p. 971</ref> It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.<ref>Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30</ref>

== Cultural Properties ==

=== National Historic Site ===
The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."<ref>{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}}</ref>

=== Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site ===
The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.<ref>Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide</ref>

=== Local Cultural Properties ===
Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.<ref>Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990</ref>

== Oto Matsuri ==
{{See|Oto Matsuri}}
The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref name=moriyasu>{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}}</ref>

== Access ==
The shrine is accessible by:
* [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス|1=Kumano Gobo Nankai Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568871}} bus to the "Court Office" stop.

The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]].

== Sacred Rock Worship Shrine ==

[[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]]
Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|qq=|1=Mount Chihogamine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405828}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].

The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship.<ref name="Kadokawa_1985_294">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294</ref>

According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.<ref>{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}}</ref>

== History ==
The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|qid=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.<ref name="Heibon_1997_191">Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191</ref>

The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.<ref>Shingū City, 1984, p. 971</ref> It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.<ref>Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30</ref>

== Cultural Properties ==

=== National Historic Site ===
The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."<ref>{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}}</ref>

=== Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site ===
The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.<ref>Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide</ref>

=== Local Cultural Properties ===
Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.<ref>Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990</ref>

== Oto Matsuri ==
{{See|Oto Matsuri}}
The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref name=moriyasu>{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}}</ref>

== Access ==
The shrine is accessible by:
* [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス|1=Kumano Gobo Nankai Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568871}} bus to the "Court Office" stop.

The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship.<ref name="Kadokawa_1985_294">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294</ref>

According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.<ref>{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}}</ref>

== History ==
The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.<ref name="Heibon_1997_191">Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191</ref>

The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.<ref>Shingū City, 1984, p. 971</ref> It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.<ref>Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30</ref>

== Cultural Properties ==

=== National Historic Site ===
The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."<ref>{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}}</ref>

=== Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site ===
The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.<ref>Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide</ref>

=== Local Cultural Properties ===
Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.<ref>Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990</ref>

== Oto Matsuri ==
{{See|Oto Matsuri}}
The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref name=moriyasu>{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}}</ref>

== Access ==
The shrine is accessible by:
* [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス|1=Kumano Gobo Nankai Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568871}} bus to the "Court Office" stop.

The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]].

== Sacred Rock Worship Shrine ==

[[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]]
Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|1=Mount Chihogamine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405828}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].

The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship.<ref name="Kadokawa_1985_294">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294</ref>

According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.<ref>{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}}</ref>

== History ==

This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.<ref name="Heibon_1997_191">Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191</ref>

The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.<ref>Shingū City, 1984, p. 971</ref> It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.<ref>Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30</ref>

The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."<ref>{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}}</ref>

The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.<ref name="Heibon_1997_191">Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191</ref>

The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.<ref>Shingū City, 1984, p. 971</ref> It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.<ref>Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30</ref>

== Cultural Properties ==

=== National Historic Site ===
The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."<ref>{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}}</ref>

=== Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site ===
The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.<ref>Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide</ref>

=== Local Cultural Properties ===
Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.<ref>Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990</ref>

== Oto Matsuri ==
{{See|Oto Matsuri}}
The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref name=moriyasu>{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}}</ref>

== Access ==
The shrine is accessible by:
* [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス|1=Kumano Gobo Nankai Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568871}} bus to the "Court Office" stop.

The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]].

== Sacred Rock Worship Shrine ==

[[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]]
Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|1=Mount Chihogamine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405828}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].

The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship.<ref name="Kadokawa_1985_294">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294</ref>

According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.<ref>{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}}</ref>

== History ==
The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.<ref name="Heibon_1997_191">Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191</ref>

The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.<ref>Shingū City, 1984, p. 971</ref> It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.<ref>Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30</ref>

== Cultural Properties ==

=== National Historic Site ===
The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."<ref>{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}}</ref>

=== Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site ===
The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.<ref>Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide</ref>

=== Local Cultural Properties ===
Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.<ref>Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990</ref>

== Oto Matsuri ==
{{See|Oto Matsuri}}
The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref name=moriyasu>{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}}</ref>

== Access ==
The shrine is accessible by:
* [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス|1=Kumano Gobo Nankai Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568871}} bus to the "Court Office" stop.

== Oto Matsuri ==
{{See|Oto Matsuri}}
The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref name=moriyasu>{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}}</ref>

== Access ==
The shrine is accessible by:
* [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス|1=Kumano Gobo Nankai Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568871}} bus to the "Court Office" stop.

| type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}
| Established = [[:en:AD 128|128 AD]] (58th year of Emperor Keikō)

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=210|coord={{coord|33|43|20.1|N|135|58|58.2|E}}}}
| location = {{ill|Shingu|WD=Q823526}}
| coordinates = {{Coord|33.72225|N|135.982833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
| deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}; {{ill|Takakuraji|WD=Q11668757}}
}}

The '''Kamikura Shrine''' (神倉神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū, Wakayama]], Japan. It is a subordinate shrine of the [[:en:Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], part of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} complex. The shrine is designated as a National Historic Site under the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]].

== Sacred Rock Worship Shrine ==

[[File:Kamikura-jinja03s2048.jpg|thumb|160px|Torii at the base of the stone steps]]
Kamikura Shrine is located on {{ill|Chihogamine|ja|千穂ヶ峯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihogamine|1=Mount Chihogamine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405828}}, a peak of Kamikura Mountain (神倉山), standing 120 meters tall. Access to the shrine requires climbing 538 steep stone steps, said to have been built during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the patronage of [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].

The shrine's main sacred object is Gotobiki Rock, a giant boulder venerated as a [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]]. Excavations around the rock have uncovered ancient relics, including pieces of [[:en:Dōtaku|dōtaku]], indicating the site's origin in [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] worship.<ref name="Kadokawa_1985_294">"Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee, 1985, p. 294</ref>

According to [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], this location is associated with the legendary [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], who received divine guidance from the god Takakuraji while traversing this area during his Eastern Expedition.<ref>{{Cite book |translator=[[Ujiya Tsutomu]] |title=Nihon Shoki (Modern Translation) |year=1988 |publisher=[[Kōdansha]] |series=[[Kodansha Academic Library]] |isbn=4-06-158833-8 |page=94}}</ref>

== History ==
The site became prominent as part of the {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, believed to be the first place where the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} deities descended. This belief reinforced its status as a spiritual center and part of the Kumano Sanzan complex.<ref name="Heibon_1997_191">Heibonsha, 1997, p. 191</ref>

The shrine has suffered multiple disasters over centuries, including fires during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, with subsequent reconstructions supported by both the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}} and local feudal lords.<ref>Shingū City, 1984, p. 971</ref> It was incorporated into the Kumano Hayatama Taisha in [[:en:1907|1907]] during the state-led modernization of religious institutions.<ref>Shingū City Education Committee, 1990, p. 30</ref>

== Cultural Properties ==

=== National Historic Site ===
The Kamikura Shrine precincts are part of the designated {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} National Historic Site and the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range."<ref>{{Cite book ja-jp|author=World Heritage Promotion Council of the Three Prefectures|year=2005|title=World Heritage Sites of the Kii Mountain Range|publisher=Council of Wakayama, Nara, and Mie Prefectures}}</ref>

=== Wakayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site ===
The 538-step stone staircase and a nearby "Shimogawa" marker stone are recognized as prefectural historic landmarks. These features highlight the region's cultural significance and the historical Kumano pilgrimage routes.<ref>Wakayama Prefecture Cultural Property Guide</ref>

=== Local Cultural Properties ===
Three relics within the precincts, including the shrine's hand-washing basin and a stone vessel, are designated as local cultural properties.<ref>Shingū City Cultural Heritage Report, 1990</ref>

== Oto Matsuri ==
{{See|Oto Matsuri}}
The {{ill|Oto Matsuri|ja|御燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oto Matsuri|qq=}} is an annual fire festival held on February 6th, featuring men carrying torches down the steep stone steps of the shrine in an exhilarating spectacle. The origins of this festival are linked to myths of Emperor [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref name=moriyasu>{{Cite journal|author=Moriyasu Toshihisa |date=2011-03 |url=https://uuair.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/4572 |title=The "Fire Festival" in Nakagawa Kenji's Works |journal=Journal of Utsunomiya University Faculty of Education |pages=17-28}}</ref>

== Access ==
The shrine is accessible by:
* [[:en:Kisei Main Line|JR Kisei Main Line]]: From [[:en:Shingū Station|Shingū Station]], it is a 15-minute walk or a short ride by {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス|1=Kumano Gobo Nankai Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11568871}} bus to the "Court Office" stop.
